data.title: Post Holdings: Double-Digit Buybacks And A Mispriced Stock
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Post Holdings (POST) fell quite a bit from their peak from a year ago, which I believe opens an opportunity to get exposure to a company with strong cash flows and double-digit buybacks, with potential to improve while the market is punishing the broader industry due to the overall weak consumer environment.

Post’s Q4’FY25 report was solid overall, with their free cash flow reaching $488.1 million despite the higher CAPEX in FY25 compared to $502.2 million in FY24 and $447.3 million in FY23, with very significant amounts spent on acquisitions.

As a note, S&P Global is quite optimistic when it comes to their reported FOCF in FY26, expecting over $650 million as a result of a mix of higher profitability, better working capital management and lower CAPEX, which could be realistic given the significant acquisitions.

For example, the ~$880 million acquisition of 8th Avenue, which they expected to add ~$86 million in annualized Adjusted EBITDA and $15 million in synergies in 2025 alone (note that they sold their pasta division for $375 million, retaining the nut butter and granola segments), while Post estimated that under average circumstances (especially lower CAPEX), they could’ve reached the equivalent of $732 million in FY25.

Even if we assume $600 million for example due to elevated CAPEX and working capital pressure, we’re still talking about a very solid ratio for a $5.13 billion market cap.

Financially, based on POST’s latest report, we see an overall good position, with the current assets covering the current liabilities well, while the debt has been increasing as a result of their expansion initiatives in recent years.

Following a recent move from Post to issue $1.3 billion in 2036 6.5% notes to repay their $1.235 billion in 2029 5.5% notes, their debt schedule is even better, with almost no debt until 2030 if we exclude the 2027 2.5% convertible notes, offering significant flexibility for nearly half a decade, in which they can repay the more expensive debt.

However, it seems counterintuitive to increase their cost of debt to repay this while they say that they’re focusing on reducing debt, as they had plenty of time to repay it with the free cash flow instead, potentially signaling that they plan to continue focusing on significant M&A, and they view the cost of paying an extra 1% as worth it while having more room to expand.

They also target enhancing their equity value through buybacks (no dividends), more M&A and even spin-offs while aiming at an Adjusted EBITDA growth rate of over 3%. In FY25, they spent $709 million on buybacks (similar to the illustrative FCF they provided above), which is a massive amount for a $5.13 billion, for a ~13.8% buyback yield, more than double the levels paid in FY24 and FY23.

Even if we assume $600 million spent on buybacks (S&P Global also expects them to spend a lot on buybacks over debt repayments), that’s a yield of about 11.7% based on the current valuation, which is very significant, while even the $488.1 million made in FY25 would put them at a yield of 9.5%.

POST is set to release their Q1'FY26 Earnings Report on February 5, and I'm looking forward to seeing the company's potential updates both for their expectations of the fiscal year given all the macro events that happened since November when they released the previous one, as well as their ongoing buybacks and potential portfolio additions or dispositions.

Risks & Opportunities

Although POST is not doing that bad, the consumer environment is still facing significant pressure, with studies highlighting the struggles caused by rising costs of living, with pressure reported especially in the US by much more than just the food industry, as consumers are shifting towards more value-oriented products - which is not as bad for Post.

Alongside that, a significant threat comes from the high costs of debt (see the recent 6.5% debt issued) as a result of the rate hikes that caused this pressure, which is a normal response to the Fed’s tool to fight inflation.

Although future rate cuts can help, keep in mind that the long-term rates that influence the SOFR are tied to much more than that, with the market taking into account the country’s growth prospects, inflation, etc., while the geopolitical situation around Greenland, the weakening dollar, rising debt, recent Government shutdown (longest in history), worries around the Fed’s independence, and more only adds extra layers of uncertainty.

Not only that, but we now see a new wave of tariff fears, with the EU preparing a response to Trump’s new tariffs due to the disagreements surrounding Greenland that would allow reciprocal tariffs and canceling the previous trade agreement, with the Union’s retaliatory tariffs on US estimated at $108 billion, while the country’s traditional allies seem to move more towards each other or even China.

As we can see, this uncertainty has significant effects even on the long-term US treasuries, with the rate on the 10-year note jumping to the highest level since August (from ~4.136% to 4.287% in less than a week). This can hurt POST’s ability to refinance over the long-term, although having almost no debt mature until 2030 offers a significant period of relief.

Recently, we saw a new study highlighting the GLP-1 threat for the food industry, finding that households that used the medicine (at least 1 individual) saw a drop in grocery spending of 5.5% six months after taking it, with an 8.6% drop for higher-income households and 4.2% for lower-income. This ultimately makes sense since that’s a main effect of the medicine, but it’s important to have quantifiable data at least.

To be fair, this may seem like a massive fundamental threat, but we have to take into account not only the number of households that will use GLP-1 (will depend on its affordability), but also the potential changes in the quality of food acquired, which the market will surely pay attention to as new data surfaces.

After using it, people are likely going to pay significantly more attention to what they eat, and the Dietary Guidelines change couldn’t come at a worse time for cereal producers, as they are now kicked all the way to the bottom of the pyramid.

The total consumption might be low as a percentage of total depending on its affordability, potentially putting even more pressure on lower-income households and deepening the K-shaped economy, which can still offer a solid market for a value-oriented business like Post.

And even then, keep in mind that companies are not static. They can adapt to these changes with new better-for-you or higher-protein product lines or even sell underperforming brands to private equity/others that can “milk” whatever is left in them and free capital to focus on other branches as a response to the environment, although the biggest risk in this regard comes from the management and their actions.

That comes on top of the potential threat from MAHA initiatives against highly processed or high sugar content food, alongside SNAP benefit changes that can continue to target some of their brands, as we are talking about potential fundamental shifts from several angles.

It’s worth noting however that a large portion of Post’s sales comes from eggs for foodservice and pet food, which are technically much safer than cereal, for example, so the potential hit on them could be significantly lower than some peers.

Besides MAHA, we saw Post being one of the 10 companies that are part of the San Francisco lawsuit against ultra-processed food producers, with the world's largest review published in late 2025 finding that ultra-processed foods have harming effects to every major organ system in the human body, adding that global corporations are driving their rise, not individual choices.

This can be only the beginning of a wider “war” against ultra-processed food in much more than just San Francisco, potentially being a major step towards broader regulation, which can threaten a big chunk of POST’s grocery segment.

Another threat I mentioned for the general food industry and legacy brands in particular comes from retailers, with giants like Target (TGT), Costco (COST), Walmart (WMT) following the lead of Europeans like Aldi or Lidl and developing their own brands.

Private labels are gaining significant ground in recent years especially since consumers are reportedly moving towards better value-oriented alternatives, which is where brands like Walmart’s Great Value can excel, basically offering a new layer of organic potential to these businesses at the expense of legacy brands.

In Post’s defense, they are the number one provider of value-oriented cereal in the US and biscuits in the UK and adding extra private label exposure to pet food following an acquisition in calendar December 2023.

At worst, they can consider selling the underperforming brands and shifting towards private label production, although the margins are not that great there, as you can imagine (bakery producers are reportedly exiting that area because of margin pressure).

Risks Reiterated

Although POST is exposed to value-oriented products that can offer a safety-net in this environment, they are still exposed to the risk of fundamental changes in long-term consumer spending/eating behavior, while political changes and the recent lawsuit can be only the beginning of wider regulatory pressure.

It’s true that they are investing significantly and are able to adapt, but we can see that not even the massive buybacks were enough to stop the stock from falling, while the rising costs of debt and competition can affect their margins and growth prospects.

Valuation

Starting with Post Holdings’ $488.1 million free cash flow in FY25, I’ll assume a more conservative $600 million in FCF in FY26 thanks to their higher profits and lower CAPEX but offset by potential tariff pressure and higher interest expenses, followed by a 5% CAGR until 2030 as we have to balance the buybacks with debt repayments/refinancing and other macro pressure factors (the market expects high-teen EPS growth over the following years thanks to the buybacks and acquisitions), followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.25% terminal growth rate, with a high 11% discount rate to be even more conservative and 62.9 million shares outstanding as of their Q4'FY25 report.

Even these conservative assumptions get us to an equity value of $7.75 billion, for an intrinsic value of $123.27 per share, which is above the current $99.33.

Conclusions

I rate POST a Buy, backed by significant potential to improve and diversify thanks to the recent acquisitions, while standing to benefit from long-term tailwinds despite potential regulatory headwinds.

The level of debt is getting a bit concerning, but their growth can more than offset it while there’s almost no maturity until 2030, and I believe that if they manage to prove S&P Global’s estimates right this year, there is certainly potential for the market’s opinion to improve.

data.title: Tesla: A Hail Mary Bet On The Musk-Trump Relationship
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) stock's trading has been noticeably divorced from the underlying company lately. As foreign competition has eaten into the company’s market share, and turned its revenue and earnings growth rates negative, TSLA has nevertheless outperformed the S&P 500 nearly 3:1 over three years. While there is some growth potential in Tesla’s smaller segments, energy and services, the company’s core auto sales business appears to be in terminal decline, with collapsing margins and rising competition. Unless the segment can grow into a viable robotaxi business–and progress on that project has been slow at best–Tesla’s auto business will probably be shrinking for some time to come.

That’s not to say that Tesla has nothing going for it. Its services business contains a valuable charging network used by many companies other than just Tesla, that is ranked #1 in the U.S. by public opinion surveys. The Chinese battery company CATL (CTATF) is basically a pure play equivalent of Tesla’s energy business, and it has a $230 billion market cap. So Tesla’s charging network and energy business have future potential. With that said, energy and services are so small as a percentage of the whole business at present (about 12% of the revenue mix each as of last quarter), they are unlikely to move the needle for the company in the near term. Thus, Tesla’s return to positive top or bottom line growth remains unlikely.

The question, then, is why has Tesla been rallying so strongly these last few years? The company’s stock has risen over 200% in the span of three short years, a period in which the underlying business has shrank. It’s possible that investors are betting on future acceleration, but it’s not clear what the catalyst for that would be: energy and services are both tiny compared to Tesla’s auto business, and the robotaxi business is rolling out extremely slowly. There is little evidence here that Tesla is a growth company in any sense of that term.

One thing is for sure: Tesla’s three year price appreciation hot streak was not due to the stock having been too cheap in the past. TSLA stock is currently unbelievably expensive, trading at 230 times earnings, 267 times forward earnings, 14.7 times sales, 15.4 times book, and 92.4 times operating cash flow. Going by these multiples, you’d think that Tesla was some kind of small cap hyper-growth stock. It is anything but.

Having ruled out current growth, near term future growth potential and valuation as possible sources for Tesla’s recent gains, we’re left with very little in fundamental terms that can justify Tesla’s valuation. The company does score well (A+) on profitability in Seeking Alpha Quant, but that’s a sector-relative score based on the consumer discretionary sector. Compared to other high flying tech stocks with P/E ratios above 100, Tesla’s margins are rather slim.

So what's really got people so optimistic about Tesla?

In this author’s view, there is one possible explanation remaining that could account for Tesla’s recent stock price performance:

Investors betting on Elon as a person rather than Tesla as a financial entity. Elon Musk has a reputation for being very friendly with President Donald Trump. It isn’t always the case that Musk and Trump love one another, but they do have an on and off relationship, one which at one point saw Musk serving as an adviser to the President while overseeing an unofficial government department, DOGE. A CEO having such a relationship can bring benefits to his companies, including government subsidies, tax breaks, and contracts. However, no such benefits have accrued to Tesla during Trump’s term to date; the administration’s Big Beautiful Bill actually ended the EV tax credit. Also, while Musk undoubtedly has a relationship with Trump, the relationship is not always a good one: the two fought a war of words with each other last year, culminating in Musk's ouster from Trump's inner circle. For these reasons, I do not think Musk’s relationship with Trump will yield many benefits for Tesla, and consider the stock a sell.

Why Tesla is Not Worth it Without Growth

In earlier paragraphs, I wrote about Tesla’s steep valuation and lack of growth. The reason why this combination is an issue is because investments have to clear opportunity costs. U.S. treasuries are considered the opportunity costs of investments, because they are close to risk free, meaning that the nominal return they offer, is highly likely to actually be delivered. This makes them useful yardsticks by which to measure other investments. The 10 year treasury yield (US10Y) is an especially popular yardstick, because 10 years is a typical time horizon for a “buy and hold” investor.

The U.S. 10 year yield when this article was written was 4.3%. If a U.S. stock’s earnings yield is less than 4.3%, then it requires future growth, because a 4.3% nominal yield is available “risk free.” In order to beat the treasury yield, the stock must either grow its earnings, or have its price come down, to a level where the earnings yield beats treasuries. Otherwise, someone buying the stock is assuming more risk for less return, which is irrational. And since nobody going long wants their asset’s price to come down, future earnings growth is really what the investor requires.

What’s Tesla’s earnings yield?

Earlier, I shared some of Tesla’s high multiples–adjusted and reported P/E ratios in the hundreds, a price/cash flow ratio above 90, etc. The inverse of these is Tesla’s earnings or cash flow “yield.” Below are displayed various such yields. As you can see, they are all much lower than the 10 year treasury yield, which means that Tesla needs to grow in the future in order to be worth the investment today.

Multiple

Implied earnings/cash flow yield

P/E (adjusted earnings)

230

0.434%

P/E (reported earnings)

301

0.33%

price/cash flow (operating cash flow)

92.4

1%

Price/FCF

197

0.5%

All of Tesla’s earnings and cash yields are much lower than the 10 year treasury yield. The yield based on reported earnings would need to increase 10 fold just to match the treasury! So, Tesla’s current valuation implies extreme amounts of future growth; a five to tenfold increase in earnings assuming that the stock price does not change.

Where the Growth Could Come From

Having established that Tesla’s current valuation implies significant future growth, we can now move on to the question of where that growth will come from. As I wrote in the introduction, the overall company has not been growing lately, so any future growth will require a catalyst. So, let’s look at the options.

The most obvious place to look is at the automotive business. Tesla's car revenue being adversely impacted by competition at the moment, declining worldwide–by high double digits in some markets. Given that EV competition is only increasing, Tesla’s auto sales revenue and margins will likely not start accelerating any time soon.

However, there is one growth initiative within Tesla’s auto segment: the robotaxi fleet. These are the autonomous vehicles that operate in cities like Austin and San Francisco. If cities across America suddenly lifted their driverless car regulations, Tesla’s robotaxi fleet would be able to grow quickly. For now, though, the regulatory environment is not kind to rapid growth in the robotaxi segment.

Then we have the energy and services segments. These segments are already growing quickly, having grown the top line 44% and 25% respectively in Q3. However, both of these segments are small as a percentage of Tesla on the whole–it will take many more years of such growth before they are big enough to start making up for weakness in the auto segment.

Finally, we have CEO Elon Musk’s relationship with Trump. This appears to be the catalyst that Tesla investors are pegging their hopes on, because it is a more plausible source of future growth for Tesla than are the two would-be catalysts mentioned above. While it’s true that Trump and Musk feuded in the past, the current nature of their relationship seems cordial. Musk recently donated $10 million to a Trump-backed candidate; Trump for his part praised Musk, calling him a super genius. So there’s some potential here.

However, Tesla did not exactly reap huge policy dividends during the last period in which Musk and Trump were friends. It was in that period that Trump let the EV tax credit expire. Trump did increase tariffs on China around the same time, but those tariffs were already so high as to keep Chinese EVs out of the U.S. market. The most obvious thing Trump did in support of Tesla last year was sing the company’s praises while displaying some of its cars on the White House lawn, but that was several quarters ago, and it did not stop Tesla’s revenue from declining. Overall, if precedent is any indication, Tesla won’t get major benefits from Elon Musk’s resumed courtship of Donald Trump.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on Tesla stock is that the potential catalysts used to justify its high multiples aren't guaranteed catalysts at all. The robotaxi fleet progress has been slow. The growth in energy and services will have to continue for a very long time to make up for the weakness in the auto business. And Musk’s relationship with Trump has never delivered much to Tesla apart from temporary gains in the stock market.

Today, trading at 230 times earnings, or a 0.434% earnings yield, Tesla needs growth in order to be worth the investment. It’s not really clear where the growth is going to come from though, at least not in the near term. For this reason I still think TSLA is a Sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Medline Industries: Solid Physical Moat With Visible Earnings Growth Runway
data.description:
Investment action

I give a buy rating for Medline Industries (MDLN) as I am positive on the vertically integrated strategy, which brings with it scale advantages in manufacturing and logistics. MDLN private label also gives it a path to expand margins as it scales. The valuation is also not extremely expensive. Even if we assume multiples to contract, if earnings grow as I expected, the upside remains attractive.

Company description

MDLN is a vertically integrated healthcare supplier founded in 1966. It manufactures or sources more than 190,000 private-label products and also distributes ~145,000 third-party SKUs. Unlike pure distributors pushing other people’s products, with its private label, MDLN controls pricing, supply, and margins across the majority of its portfolio. It also has a strong distribution arm that serves the full continuum of care, meaning MDLN is already “entrenched” in most acute care hospital systems, as they are in ambulatory surgery centers, physician offices, and long-term care facilities. This provides a natural hedge against shifts in patient volume. Even if care migrates from hospitals to outpatient settings, MDLN can still capture the revenue regardless of the site of service.

Moat

The strongest moat that MDLN has is its physical infrastructure. Over the years, MDLN has invested resources to build a logistics network subscale players cannot match or replicate easily. To put things in perspective, MDLN operates 69 distribution centers across more than 29 million square feet. These centers are supported by MedTrans, MDLN’s private fleet of >2,000 trucks. My view is the player that owns the last mile has the more durable demand profile because this is a market that values reliability as much as price. And the reason is simple. Hospitals operate on precise schedules, where the right equipment must be available at the right time for, say, a surgical procedure to happen. If the hospital doesn’t have any of the right medicine in place, for instance, it could be forced to cancel a day’s worth of procedures.

From a financial perspective, that downtime translates directly into lost high-margin revenue. Outside of the hospital’s own negligence in placing orders, a high-risk area that could cause such an event is the logistics, i.e., late deliveries by the provider. In this sense, owning the fleet means MDLN has control over the movement of goods from the warehouse to the hospital, and this distinction is what, I believe, allows MDLN to meet the hospital’s service standard requirements. Over time, as MDLN continuously shows that it is more reliable, it should gradually consolidate wallet share within the hospital, as deal renewals or new deals tend to come under prime vendor agreements.

When MDLN becomes the logistic provider, it opens up the pathway for cross-selling. Because it owns both the customer relationship and the delivery route, it makes a lot of sense for customers to shift purchasing toward MDLN Brand products. MDLN could easily incentivize hospitals to do this by subsidizing the cost (since it is being paid for the distribution as well, and also private label has higher margins). This is of course not going to happen at one go since the hospitals are under contract with other providers. But as contracts renew, the opportunity will come for MDLN to displace competitors.

The overall result is a virtuous cycle. Logistics helps MDLN win the account; control of the account enables MDLN to cross-sell, and that drives up overall margins, which MDLN can use to subsidize either its distribution or private-label arm to win more accounts. Competitors without manufacturing scale or a private fleet of trucks cannot replicate this.

Revenue path/outlook

I think one of the reasons why the stock is not trading where it is today is because of a near-term issue, which is tariffs. Management has quantified the impact at ~$325 million for 2025, and that may have spooked some investors into staying on the sidelines for the near term. I won’t say that move is wrong, but I believe tariffs are a temporary earnings drag and are nothing structural. The reality is underlying demand remains very strong, and we can see this in the new business wins numbers. As of Q3 2025, MDLN had already signed $2.1 billion in new Prime Vendor contracts for FY2025, and for context, that is ~2x MDLN’s historical annual run rate. Note that these are not pipeline metrics; they are signed agreements with clients that have replaced incumbent distributors. Think about the implication here. MDLN is winning a significant amount of demand vs. the past and is gaining market share, both of which align with my view that this is a virtuous cycle happening.

Some may see the fact that Prime Vendor contracts do not convert all at once as a problem, but I don’t think so. Volume will only ramp up as hospitals onboard systems and transition logistics. Which means this is going to be a multi-year revenue build, which means that MDLN’s near-term revenue growth is already visible, and that de-risks the entire growth outlook (this is even before factoring in any additional wins).

Remember the point I made about MDLN cross-selling? That should become apparent in the near future too, and we can get a sense of the magnitude. Management noted that MDLN brand penetration often starts at ~13%. Over time, as contracts mature, private-label penetration can move toward 50% or even 60%. If that is true, that mix shift will create a large profit opportunity without the need for incremental selling expense. The estimated opportunity here is more than $1 billion in gross profit across the existing contract base. $1 billion is ~14% of FY24 gross profit, for comparison.

Valuation

Summing up, I think MDLN's moat gives it a durable growth runway, and the record contract wins drive volume leverage, which is boosted further with private-label conversion. My model assumes MDLN can replicate its current 10% growth for the foreseeable future and margin to expand modestly for reasons I mentioned above. One area I am more conservative is multiples. MDLN trades at ~30x NTM PE today, a premium to even large players like Thermo Fisher (TMO), which trades at 25x with high-single-digit growth and a 16% net margin. Just simply assuming multiples will converge to TMO levels, the upside is attractive enough to justify a long.

Risk

MDLN’s ownership structure creates an overhang. With the Mills family and PE sponsors controlling ~88% of the vote, future exits could prevent new investors from coming on board. Fundamentally, customer consolidation is a threat for MDLN. When hospital systems merge, they tend to have more bargaining power, which could squeeze MDLN’s distribution margins. The long-term impact of GLP-1s on surgical volumes is unknown now. If it works well, procedure growth could structurally decelerate.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for MDLN. The solid physical moat, market share gains, and a proven private-label conversion model give confidence that growth is durable and margin can expand. Tariffs may weigh on near-term results, but they do not change the longer-term outlook.

data.title: Kosmos Energy: Attempting To Work Its Way Out Of Financial Distress
data.description:
Kosmos (KOS) is an offshore producer that has been in a financially tough spot. The last article noted the financial distress and advised readers to stay away from this stock. So far so good as the stock is down considerably from the last article. The general advice has got to be that a relatively high debt ratio does not work in this industry. You can go to my profile and see all the tombstones of what were high debt companies that were going to climb their way out of debt with a surefire strategy. But oil and gas is very volatile and low visibility. Most high debt companies like this one need cooperation from commodity prices to emerge from the debt straitjacket. This company is running into weak oil prices instead.

One of the hallmarks of a potentially successful turnaround in this industry is a high debt rating. For some small companies, it is a very low debt ratio because low debt ratio companies (which include companies with high debt ratings) rarely get into serious trouble.

On the other hand, a high debt company can fail for reasons beyond the control of management. This is the same situation in which the management of a low debt company "sits back and cashes checks" while waiting for the next inevitable cyclical recovery.

Stock Price

The stock price of this company indicates that this company needs considerable commodity price cooperation. If history is any guide, it is not going to get that consideration.

The stock has lost over 25% of its value since the last article was written back in October. But that is just the latest in what has been a really bad year for any long-term holder of the stock.

Debt

The stock price is in a range that indicates more than a fair amount of financial distress.

The reason for the low stock price is shown above. The debt due schedule is extremely challenging at a time when commodity prices are weakening. This is an unfortunate situation that often occurs with high debt ratio companies.

These companies often need a decent period of cooperative commodity prices. But they do not get it.

The latest announcement about tendering for the remaining debt due in 2026 while working on the 2027 debt is overshadowed by the fact that this debt simply goes into another debt instrument.

Drilling

The same announcement about the debt has also announced that the second well of the drilling campaign has been successful and it should begin producing more than 10,000 BOED. That production is sorely needed given the debt due shown previously.

Clearly the stock price indicates worries about the value of all the production compared to the debt. There is hedging. But small changes in the price received often cause much larger income changes because income is usually a small percentage of revenue (relatively speaking).

What this company needs are much higher commodity prices, and it likely needs those higher prices for several years. Rarely do companies in this situation get what they need.

Fourth Quarter Guidance Not Achieved

Here was the original fourth quarter guidance:

The fourth quarter production announced in the latest press release was 59,000 BOED. While that can easily happen in the oil and gas business, financial constraints may be a bit tight for that to be an allowable happening. Now production for the first quarter is expected to be in the 70,000 BOED that is at the high end of the fourth quarter range. Additionally, there are still more wells to be drilled.

Offshore producers can sometimes drill their way out of a tough spot. If that happens, then the free cash flow should be used to repay debt down to a very low level (meaning small ratio). Debt and the offshore business do not get along well.

This well may possibly be what made the Nordic bond offering a reality. The company appears to be finding a way to pay down at least some of the 2027 debt (although the costs involved would likely push the total debt higher still). However, the key idea right now is to get some breathing room to give the management recovery strategy time to work.

Summary

Kosmos is in no less financial distress than it was when the last article was written. So far, management has two tangible successes. The announcement of a second well drilled and completed that was successful combined with the latest bond offering notice to push some of the debt due back until there is cash flow to deal with the debt. This is only talking about the latest announcements. But it does indicate some progress in dealing with a particularly urgent situation.

For investors, it is best to watch the progress from the sidelines. In particular, the market has shown a disdain for debt that has elsewhere held back stock price progress until the market saw a debt ratio (and accompanying free cash flow) that it liked. The market has long emphasized an ability to repay debt using conservative commodity price assumptions ever since the particularly challenging period of 2015-2020.

This is the period that began with the "great price decline" in oil and ended with covid. There was not a whole lot of good news inbetween. It marked the end of rapid growth of unconventional oil and gas production combined with the beginning of shareholder returns demand by the market.

This stock remains a sell at the current price. The stock has declined materially all year. But the key idea here is that the stock price is currently unlikely to "go anywhere" while weak commodity prices and political uncertainty predominate.

To me, the amount of debt, the debt due schedule, and the current industry situation all argue against an investment at the current time. This is for very well-trained investors who are very experienced in a situation like this. It is not for the average investor.

Risks

Some combination of drilling success and higher commodity prices is needed for a period of time to allow the debt ratio to come down. Companies that needed this (especially offshore companies) rarely get what they need to turn the situation around. Therefore, it is best to watch this situation from the sidelines until a safer set of investment success criteria replace what is there now.

Risk reduction to avoid a pile of debt with insufficient production is an essential part of the business. There has to be some evidence in the future that management controls risk so that this situation never occurs again.

Turnaround managements are a relatively rare breed in really any industry. Replacement of a loss of services can prove to be particularly daunting. In fact, it could not only set back the future of the company. Such a situation could prove to be fatal to the company's future prospects.

data.title: IBM Was 'Boring' - It's Now An AI Growth Machine
data.description:
Investment Thesis

“This is a new IBM.”

That is my five-letter advice to any investor who is aware of the inferiority of International Business Machines Corporation or IBM’s (IBM) share performance over the past 2 decades. Past returns also meant IBM’s shares were associated with being a “boring” investment.

This IBM, under current management, is swift, agile, nimble, and not afraid to switch things up—something that is extremely crucial in the age of AI, where demand cycles move at lightning speed.

For a company the size of IBM, it is exciting to see how quickly Big Blue moved to compete in the post-ChatGPT era of large language model, or LLM, wars. Now that the LLM space appears to be becoming heavily contested and commoditized, IBM is again changing its approach to compete for a growing share of high-margin enterprise IT budgets, transitioning to the platform layer of AI—agentic data. The company is doing this without taking its eyes off the pedal of its free cash engine.

I continue to maintain my bullish view on IBM and explain my thesis below.

IBM’s New AI Strategy Targets The Real Currency Of AI

IBM currently has two growth ramps that are driving the company towards becoming a beneficiary of emerging technologies—AI & Quantum. I discussed IBM’s quantum compute strategy in August last year, and the shares are up ~40% since then.

Over the past 2-3 months I have noticed Big Blue making quiet but strong moves towards shifting their approach towards AI.

In the post-ChatGPT era, so far, IBM’s approach towards AI has been to compete in the market for frontier LLMs by leveraging an open-source strategy, particularly through its Granite series of models. The open-source strategy was based on its Red Hat open-source platform, which has been one of IBM’s most transformative acquisitions thus far, in my view. Apart from IBM, Meta Platforms (META) was the other significant player that competed in the LLM landscape via an open-source adoption strategy.

That strategy worked well for IBM, as seen in the chart below, where IBM Software, the business segment that manages the development of products and platforms for most AI initiatives, went from growing at a low-to-mid single-digit pace to a high single-digit pace. The acceleration in IBM’s Software segment also helped Big Blue grow at a mid-high single-digit quarterly growth pace, much better than the low-mid single-digit growth pace between 2023 and 2024. (The current growth pace impressively eclipses the period of contracting revenues in the previous decade.)

However, in the past 15-18 months, competition in the LLM landscape has become fierce, leading to heavy commoditization and a significant drop in LLM inference prices. In such situations, open-source approaches might yield desired results, pushing leading players to rethink the open-source approach and/or the pursuit of LLM dominance altogether.

I believe IBM’s management understood the trade-off between staying committed on their increasingly competitive journey towards LLM dominance and finding newer higher-margin, value play areas where they could stay competitive within the broader LLM landscape. This probably explains why IBM’s exec team switched tactics towards competing in the platform ecosystem for LLMs and made two acquisitions in the automation and data platform end market for LLMs along with the HashiCorp acquisition last year.

In the chart below, I break out IBM’s Software segment by its subsegments and plot the annual growth rates per quarter for each Software subsegment. Investors will notice two things.

First, both the acquisitions that IBM made in the last 18 months were shrewd additions to the company, accretive to the company’s overall growth, as seen above. Second, the timing of both these acquisitions appears to be almost perfect, with the company quickly folding the acquisitions into the company’s Software business segment, rolling it out to customers, and rapidly benefiting from the synergy of these acquisitions made.

I believe IBM’s management may have been satisfied with how synergetic the two 2024 acquisitions were to IBM Software’s Data business. But the fact that growth was not able to match up with the teens' growth rates seen in Red Hat and, especially, in Automation, may have pushed management to re-energize their Data platform with the recent acquisition of data streaming product company Confluent. In my view, this is a clever acquisition by IBM’s management because it will allow the company to address the data streaming and orchestration market that will become more important as the competition and complexity at the LLM layer proliferate further. IBM is now appearing to target the exabytes of data being ingested by LLMs, which is the real currency for LLMs.

I suspect IBM fully understands the extent to which the company can sell to strong demand and price to value based on the synergy of its Confluent acquisition. This is why management already divulged that they expect the Confluent acquisition to be “accretive to adjusted EBITDA within the first full year, and free cash flow in year two, post close.”

IBM’s recent history suggests that management usually has a good track record with its acquisitions. If we factor in management’s guidance from the Confluent acquisition at face value, then it's highly likely that IBM’s margins will continue to soar despite the company delivering to investors their highest-ever margin profile.

That robust margin expansion has allowed IBM to deliver three successive years of free cash growth. Currently, the company is on track to deliver >5% free cash growth, amounting to ~$14B. Many investors may still shy away from the single-digit growth rates in IBM’s revenue and free cash. Rather than focusing on the digit growth rates, I would implore investors to focus on the acceleration or accretion, which creates incremental investability in IBM’s shares.

My take—that only means there likely is a stronger update coming in IBM’s FCF outlook next week when the company reports Q4 CY25 earnings or the next quarterly earnings reports.

IBM’s Valuation Still Points To Upside

As detailed in one of my earlier coverages on IBM, I will continue to believe that IBM can deliver free cash growth rates of 5.9%. I am mostly inclined to upgrade this outlook if the recent acquisitions are accretive to free cash growth, but I will wait for management’s outlook and forward-looking views from next week’s ER (January 28th).

Presently, markets are pricing IBM’s shares in a manner that is consistent with expectations of 4.8% free cash growth, per my reverse DCF model. Since I have already stated my expectations of 5.9% free cash growth, my reverse DCF model prices IBM’s shares at ~$329 per share. The reverse DCF assumes a discount rate of 7.6%.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

IBM’s new strategy to gradually pivot away from a focus on LLMs towards the data and AI platform ecosystem is not entirely new, but there is a risk the strategy may not pay off if competition in the space significantly rises from here. Apart from DataBricks, Snowflake (SNOW) and other vendors such as Amazon (AMZN) AWS’s Kinesis and Alphabet (GOOG) Google Cloud’s Dataflow are in this space. IBM’s first focus would be to expand its current book of business of $9.5B since inception by selling to its current installed base of customers.

With Q4 ER on tap next week, here’s what investors can expect:

  • Q4 EPS of $4.3 growing 9.5% on Q4 revenues of $19.23B growing 9.6%. That implies a full-year CY25 EPS of $11.35, up 9.9% on CY25 revenues of $67.1B, up 6.7%.
  • For CY26, markets are expecting a slowdown in revenues of 4.5% growth to $70.2B, which is conservative, in my opinion.
  • IBM Consulting/Managed Services will be another area to watch for investors to understand if the industry slowdown is still likely to persist in the new year.

Takeaway

With another widely expected year of revenue acceleration, IBM has emerged from its decades-long shadow of revenue contractions, firmly capitalizing on the strong demand it sees for its AI platform. The gradual move away from competing in LLMs to being the platform provider marks a clever shift for the company, positioning itself to deliver free cash growth in the face of structural AI demand.

I continue to reiterate my bullishness for IBM’s shares.

data.title: Deciding 'When' To Buy
data.description:
This is an educational article. All examples are for educational purposes and are not a direct commendation except as stated.

Deciding “what to buy” is different than deciding “when” to buy it. Warren Buffett highlighted this when he said: “A simple rule dictates my buying. Be fearful when others are greedy, and be greedy when others are fearful.” Notice his “when” is based on investor emotions not economic data.

Yet, we know his decisions on “what to buy” are based on analyzing a company’s economic situation compared to its current value or price.

It’s important to understand these are two separate and distinct decisions.

Opinion: I want to make something clear. Deciding “what to buy” should be determined by careful analysis of a company’s industry and the economics of that company. That’s what most of the analysis at Seeking Alpha is about. However, in the area of deciding “when to buy” fundamental analysis is not very good and investor sentiment is by far the better tool.

Investor Sentiment

Investor “fear” and “greed” falls under the broad category of investor sentiment. Another comparable term is “investor expectations.”

There are numerous ways to measure investor sentiment or expectations in an individual stock but by far the best way is through puts and calls data. It's a direct way to measure how bullish or bearish investors are about that stock.

Our analysis compares the amount of money going into directional puts and calls over twenty days. This time frame gives the data a long term perspective.

We then put the results on the Sentiment King Red Zone Green Zone ranking method, which graphically shows when bullish or bearish expectations are at extremes. I recently wrote about it in this article (New option indicators are signaling higher prices).

Example: T-Mobile

Now the example company I'm going to use in the article is T-Mobile (TMUS). Here is its seven-year chart. As you can see, it had a serious setback last year.

Suppose, after careful economic analysis of T-Mobile and its sector, which is telecommunications, one has decided that this is the company they want to buy for the long term. Question: Is this the right time to buy it?

It’s answering this question where investor sentiment can help the analyst.

We’re first going to look at investor sentiment in the telecommunications sector. Then we’re going to look at investor sentiment in T-Mobile itself.

Sectors

First, let's take a look at the sector, which is telecommunications. Here's a chart of how the sector ranks on our sentiment scale for the major S&P sectors.

We’ve circled telecommunications in red. Notice that the telecommunications sector ranks as the second most extreme in investor expectations.

It's always positive when you have extreme bearish expectations. This is why Buffett likes to buy when investors are negative or fearful.

The Telecommunications Sector

This next chart gives a closer look at why this sector registers at one of the most extreme Green Zone readings.

The price chart graphs the State Street telecommunications ETF. The bottom curve is our ranking of the directional puts and calls ratio over 20 days of all 49 stocks that make up this sector.

Notice how past Green Zone readings were good moments to buy the sector and bad moments to sell it. Likewise, Red Zone areas were good moments to generally sell or moments to wait for a better time to buy it.

It's important to note that sentiment is an intermediate term indicator, not a long term forecasting tool. The directional premium puts to call ratio generally produces signals every few months.

So this chart tells an analyst, if they're suddenly optimistic about prospects for telecommunications, that now is a good time to buy the sector.

It also tells an analyst if they're negative on the sector, they should probably wait before they sell a stock in that sector. That they most likely will get a higher price to sell at.

Investor Sentiment In T-Mobile

How is investor sentiment in T-Mobile itself? Here is a corresponding chart for T-Mobile. The bottom curve ranks the directed puts to calls premium ratio over the last 20 days.

While there are exceptions, Green Zone readings were generally good buying opportunities while red zone readings were good selling opportunities.

After the yearlong decline, investor sentiment is the most negative it’s been in years. For investors who are bullish on T-Mobile, this indicates that it's a good time to purchase.

For those investors who are negative on the outlook for T-Mobile, it means that they should wait and get a better price before they sell.

Do You Always Wait For "Fear" Or "Greed" To Act?

Does an investor only act when there's greed or fear? Does Warren Buffett only act when there's greed or fear? No. He just buys more when there is fear and buys less when there is greed. Remember, the extreme moments he's referring to don't occur that often.

When sentiment is not at an extreme, which is most of the time, and an investor is bullish or bearish on a stock, they should go ahead and act. You don't have to wait for extreme sentiment, a Red Zone or Green Zone signal, to buy or sell.

However, if sentiment is at an extreme, we think you should act or wait to get a better price depending on the situation.

Summary

So, I would never buy a stock I was optimistic about without first looking at investor sentiment to see if I might get a better price if I wait to buy.

Likewise, I would never sell a company I was negative on without first looking at whether it's in the Green Zone, and I'll probably get a better price if I wait there too.

Sentiment can help an investor who has decided to buy or sell get the best price that aligns with their decision.

data.title: Nomura High Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

US high yield bonds, as measured by the ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index, returned +1.35% for 4Q25, while European and global high yield bonds returned +0.52% and +1.32%, as measured by the ICE BofA Euro High Yield Index and the ICE BofA US Dollar Global High Yield Index, respectively.

The ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index returned +0.20% in October as concerns about third-quarter earnings reports and a possible US government shutdown triggered a flight to quality for high yield market participants. Given the shift in investor risk appetite, BB-rated securities outperformed lower-quality B-rated and CCC-rated securities. BB-rated securities returned +0.50% for the quarter versus declines of 0.06% and 0.37% for B-rated and CCC-rated securities, respectively.

High yield market volatility continued into November as the US government shutdown and the increased likelihood of US Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts resulted in another strong quarter of performance from the higher-quality component of the high yield market. In November, the ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index returned +0.50% as BB-rated securities returned +0.63% followed by B-rated securities returning +0.70% and CCC-rated securities returning declining 0.76%. This was the first time since October 2023 in which CCC-rated bonds declined two consecutive months.

December closed the year strong as positive economic data points, a dovish Fed, and robust high yield capital markets activity provided the backdrop for risk assets to perform well. CCC-rated bonds ended their two-month streak of negative returns by returning +0.72% for the month. B-rated bonds also performed very well, leading the way with a +0.90% return for the month followed by BB-rated bonds returning 0.47% for the same period.

For the quarter, BB-rated bonds outperformed, returning +1.58%, while B-rated and CCC-rated bonds returned +1.55% and -0.42%, respectively. The leading sectors were broadcasting (+3.57%), telecommunications (+2.74%), and metals/mining (+2.67%). The weakest-performing sectors were retail (-0.75%), media (-0.67%), and paper/packaging (-0.62%).

New-issue volume totaled $65.4 billion for the quarter. For the full year 2025, refinancings comprised 70.4% of new-issue activity, followed by mergers and acquisitions at 13.9% and general corporate purposes at 14.8%. The 12-month par-weighted US high yield default rate ended the quarter at 1.88% and the outlook for the 2026 default rate ranges from 1.75% to 2.25%.

Sources: Bloomberg and J.P. Morgan, unless otherwise noted.

Within the Fund

For 4Q25, Nomura High Income Fund Institutional Class (IVHIX) shares slightly underperformed the Fund’s benchmark, the ICE BofA US High Yield Constrained Index.

Key contributors to performance included:

  • Security selection within the media sector
  • Security selection within the retail sector
  • Security selection within the automotive sector
  • Security selection within the banking sector

Key detractors from performance included:

  • Security selection within the leisure sector
  • An underweight to the real estate sector
  • An underweight to the healthcare sector
  • Security selection within the consumer goods sector

Outlook

Looking forward, we expect potential bouts of volatility. Earnings could come under stress if tariff-related issues and weaker consumer spending weigh on corporate margins and free cash flow profiles. Nevertheless, solid corporate balance sheets, low default rates, and attractive yields should help mitigate upside risks to yields and spreads within the high yield market. We will continue to rely on our disciplined, bottom-up approach to capitalize on opportunities as valuations warrant.

For Class A shares, a 1% contingent deferred sales charge (CDSC) is only imposed on certain Class A shares that are purchased at net asset value (NAV) for $1 million or more that are subsequently redeemed within 18 months of purchase.

The performance quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. Performance data current to the most recent month end may be obtained by calling 800 523-1918 or visiting nomuraassetmanagement.com/performance.

Total returns may reflect waivers and/or expense reimbursements by the manager and/or distributor (as applicable) for some or all of the periods shown. Performance would have been lower without such waivers and reimbursements.

Performance at NAV assumes that no front-end sales charge applied. Performance at offer assumes that a front-end sales charge applied to the extent applicable. As of December 1, 2025, Nomura Holding America Inc. completed the acquisition of Macquarie Asset Management’s US and European public investments business.

Carefully consider the Fund’s investment objectives, risk factors, charges, and expenses before investing. This and other information can be found in the Fund’s prospectus and its summary prospectus, which may be obtained by visiting nomuraassetmanagement.com/literature or calling 800 523-1918. Investors should read the prospectus and the summary prospectus carefully before investing.

The views expressed represent the investment team’s assessment of the Fund and market environment as of the date indicated, and should not be considered a recommendation to buy, hold, or sell any security, and should not be relied on as research or investment advice.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal.

A fund or strategy may not receive payment of principal, interest, and other amounts due in connection with investments in loans and other direct indebtedness. Furthermore, loans and other direct indebtedness may be less liquid than other investments and may not be considered securities that are subject to the protections of securities laws.

Fixed income securities can lose value, including the possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities are subject to credit risk and interest rate risk. Credit risk is the risk that an issuer of a fixed income security may be unable to make interest payments and/or repay principal in a timely manner. Interest rate risk is the risk that prices of bonds and other fixed income securities will increase as interest rates fall and decrease as interest rates rise. Fixed income securities with longer maturities or duration generally are more sensitive to interest rate changes. • Fluctuations in exchange rates between currencies may cause the value of an investment to decline. Some currencies may have low liquidity which may prevent the fund from effecting positions at favorable rates, thus subjecting the fund or strategy to substantial losses. • Foreign securities (particularly in emerging markets) may be adversely affected by political instability, changes in currency exchange rates, inefficient markets and higher transaction costs, foreign economic conditions, the imposition of economic or trade sanctions, or inadequate or different regulatory and accounting standards. • High yield securities (“junk bonds”) are subject to reduced creditworthiness of issuers, increased risk of default, and a more limited and less liquid secondary market. High yield securities may also be subject to greater price volatility and risk of loss of income and principal than higher-rated securities. • The fund or strategy is subject to liquidity risk, which is the risk that the fund or strategy’s investments cannot be readily sold within seven calendar days at approximately the price at which the fund or strategy has valued them. • The principal on a fixed income security may be prepaid prior to maturity, which may require reinvestment at a lower interest rate. • Restricted securities may be less liquid and harder to value than unrestricted securities.

Index performance returns do not reflect any management fees, transaction costs, or expenses. Indices are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. The ICE BofA US High Yield Constrained Index tracks the performance of US dollar-denominated high yield corporate debt publicly issued in the US domestic market, but caps individual issuer exposure at 2% of the benchmark. The ICE BofA Euro High Yield Index tracks the performance of euro-denominated below-investment-grade corporate debt publicly issued in the euro domestic or Eurobond markets. The ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index tracks the performance of US dollar-denominated below-investment-grade corporate debt, currently in a coupon paying period, that is publicly issued in the US domestic market. Qualifying securities must have at least 18 months to final maturity at the time of issuance, at least one-year remaining term to final maturity as of the rebalancing date, a fixed coupon schedule, and a minimum amount outstanding of $250 million. The ICE BofA US Dollar Global High Yield Index tracks the performance of US dollar-denominated below-investment-grade corporate debt publicly issued in the US domestic and Eurobond markets.

This document may mention bond ratings published by nationally recognized statistical rating organizations (NRSROs) Standard & Poor’s, Moody’s Investors Service, and Fitch, Inc. Bonds rated AAA are rated as having the highest quality and are generally considered to have the lowest degree of investment risk. Bonds rated AA are considered to be of high quality, but with a slightly higher degree of risk than bonds rated AAA. Bonds rated A are considered to have many favorable investment qualities, though they are somewhat more susceptible to adverse economic conditions. Bonds rated BBB are believed to be of medium-grade quality and generally riskier over the long term. Bonds rated BB, B, and CCC are regarded as having significant speculative characteristics, with BB indicating the least degree of speculation of the three.

Institutional Class shares, Class R shares, Class R6 shares, and Class Y shares are available only to certain investors. See the prospectus for more information. Nothing presented should be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell any security or follow any investment technique or strategy.

All third-party marks cited are the property of their respective owners.

Nomura Asset Management is part of the Investment Management Division of the Nomura Group, providing integrated public and private market asset management services across equities, fixed income, private credit and multi-asset solutions to intermediary and institutional clients. Nomura Asset Management primarily operates through several distinct investment managers, which includes Nomura Investment Management Business Trust (NIMBT), a Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) registered investment adviser. Investment advisory services are provided to the Nomura Funds by Delaware Management Company, a series of NIMBT. The Nomura Funds are distributed by Delaware Distributors, L.P., a registered broker/dealer and member of the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) and an affiliate of NIMBT.

Document must be used in its entirety.
